revision.text: #REDIRECT [[位階]]

'''正六位'''(しょうろくい)とは、日本の[[位階]]及び[[神階]]における位のひとつ。[[従五位]]の下、[[従六位]]の上に位する。[[勲等]]では勲五等に、[[金鵄勲章|功級]]では功五級に相当。
==正六位==
律令制下において六位は下国の国司及び国府の次官である介が叙せられる位であった。地下人の位階とされ、五位以上の貴族とは一線を画する位階であり、昇殿は許されなかった。 但し、蔵人の場合、その職務上、六位であっても昇殿が許され五位以上の者、六位蔵人の者を合わせて殿上人と称した。 明治時代以降は、少佐の階級にある者などがこの位に叙せられた。また、今日では[[警察官]]では[[警視正]]、[[消防吏員]]では[[消防監]]などがこの位に叙せられる他、[[市町村議会]][[議長]]にあった者、特別施設や学校創立者その他、業種等で功労ある者などが没後に叙せられる。
==関連項目==
* [[正一位]] * [[従一位]] * [[正二位]] * [[従二位]] * [[正三位]] * [[従三位]] * [[正四位]] * [[従四位]] * [[正五位]] * [[従五位]] * [[従六位]] * [[正七位]] * [[従七位]] * [[正八位]] * [[従八位]]
==外部リンク== *「[http://law.e-gov.go.jp/htmldata/T15/T15CO325.html 位階令]」
[[Category:位階|しゆこい]] [[Category:日本の律令制|しゆこい]]

'''正六位'''(しょうろくい)とは、日本の[[位階]]及び[[神階]]における位のひとつ。[[従五位]]の下、[[従六位]]の上に位する。[[勲等]]では勲五等に、[[金鵄勲章|功級]]では功五級に相当。
==解説==
律令制下において六位は下国の国司及び国府の次官である介が叙せられる位であった。地下人の位階とされ、五位以上の貴族とは一線を画する位階であり、昇殿は許されなかった。但し、蔵人の場合、その職務上、六位であっても昇殿が許され五位以上の者、六位蔵人の者を合わせて殿上人と称した。
神階においては、正六位が最下位となる。
明治時代以降は、少佐の階級にある者などがこの位に叙せられた。また、今日では[[警察官]]では[[警視正]]、[[消防吏員]]では[[消防監]]などがこの位に叙せられる他、[[市町村議会]][[議長]]にあった者、特別施設や学校創立者その他、業種等で功労ある者などが没後に叙せられる。
{{日本の位階}}

'''正六位'''（しょうろくい）とは、日本の[[位階]]及び[[神階]]における位のひとつ。[[従五位]]の下、[[従六位]]の上に位する。[[勲等]]では勲五等に、[[金鵄勲章|功級]]では功五級に相当。
[[律令制]]下において六位は下国の[[国司]]及び[[国府]]の次官である介が叙せられる位であった。[[地下人]]の位階とされ、五位以上の[[貴族]]（通貴）とは一線を画する位階であり[[昇殿]]は許されなかった。但し、[[蔵人]]の場合、その職務上、六位であっても昇殿が許され、五位以上の者と[[六位蔵人]]の者を合わせて[[殿上人]]と称した。神階においては、正六位が最下位となる。
[[明治時代]]以降は、[[少佐]]の階級にある者などがこの位に叙せられた。また、今日では[[警察官]]では[[警視正]]、[[消防吏員]]では[[消防監]]などがこの位に叙せられる他、[[市町村議会]][[議長]]にあった者、特別施設や学校創立者その他、業種等で功労ある者などが没後に叙せられる。
{{日本の位階}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年8月}} '''正六位'''（しょうろくい）とは、[[日本]]の[[位階]]及び[[神階]]における位のひとつ。[[従五位]]の下、[[従六位]]の上に位する。[[勲等]]では勲五等に、[[金鵄勲章|功級]]では功五級に相当。
[[律令制]]下において六位は下国の[[国司]]及び[[国府]]の次官である介が叙せられる位であった。[[地下人]]の位階とされ、五位以上の[[貴族]]（通貴）とは一線を画する位階であり[[昇殿]]は許されなかった。ただし、[[蔵人]]の場合、その職務上、六位であっても昇殿が許され、五位以上の者と[[六位蔵人]]の者を合わせて[[殿上人]]と称した。神階においては、正六位が最下位となる。[[侍]]は朝廷の実務を担い貴族や諸大夫に仕える、位階六位どまりの侍品（さむらいほん）が元である。六位層は[[袍]]が[[縹]]色であることから、後には[[青侍]]と呼ばれた。
[[明治時代]]以降は、[[少佐]]の階級にある者などがこの位に叙せられた。
今日では[[日本の警察官|警察官]]では[[警視正]]、[[消防吏員]]では[[消防監]]などがこの位に叙せられる他、[[日本の地方議会|市町村議会]][[議長]]にあった者、特別施設や学校創立者、或いは芸術家など業種等で功労ある者などが没後に叙せられる。
==外部リンク== * [https://elaws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - [[e-Gov法令検索]] * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - 国立国会図書館近代デジタルライブラリー
{{日本の位階}} {{デフォルトソート:しようろくい}} [[Category:位階制度|しよう6い]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年8月}} '''正六位'''（しょうろくい）とは、[[日本]]の[[位階]]及び[[神階]]における位のひとつ。[[従五位]]の下、[[従六位]]の上に位する。[[勲等]]では勲五等に、[[金鵄勲章|功級]]では功五級に相当。
[[律令制]]下において六位は下国の[[国司]]及び[[国府]]の次官である介が叙せられる位であった。[[地下人]]の位階とされ、五位以上の[[貴族]]（通貴）とは一線を画する位階であり[[昇殿]]は許されなかった。ただし、[[蔵人]]の場合、その職務上、六位であっても昇殿が許され、五位以上の者と[[六位蔵人]]の者を合わせて[[殿上人]]と称した。神階においては、正六位が最下位となる。[[侍]]は朝廷の実務を担い貴族や諸大夫に仕える、位階六位どまりの侍品（さむらいほん）が元である。六位層は[[袍]]が[[縹]]色であることから、後には[[青侍]]と呼ばれた。
[[明治時代]]以降は、[[少佐]]の階級にある者などがこの位に叙せられた。
今日では[[日本の警察官|警察官]]では[[警視正]]、[[消防吏員]]では[[消防監]]などがこの位に叙せられる他、[[日本の地方議会|市町村議会]][[議長]]にあった者、特別施設や学校創立者、あるいは芸術家など業種等で功労ある者などが没後に叙せられる。
==外部リンク== * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - [[e-Gov法令検索]] * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - 国立国会図書館近代デジタルライブラリー
{{日本の位階}} {{デフォルトソート:しようろくい}} [[Category:位階制度|しよう6い]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (ranking of deities)|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Jūgoi|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Jūrokui|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (ranking of deities)|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive
{{日本の位階}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

revision.comment: 外部リンクの修正 (laws.e-gov.go.jp) ([[Wikipedia:Bot|Bot]]による編集)
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年8月}} '''正六位'''（しょうろくい）とは、[[日本]]の[[位階]]及び[[神階]]における位のひとつ。[[従五位]]の下、[[従六位]]の上に位する。[[勲等]]では勲五等に、[[金鵄勲章|功級]]では功五級に相当。
[[律令制]]下において六位は下国の[[国司]]及び[[国府]]の次官である介が叙せられる位であった。[[地下人]]の位階とされ、五位以上の[[貴族]]（通貴）とは一線を画する位階であり[[昇殿]]は許されなかった。ただし、[[蔵人]]の場合、その職務上、六位であっても昇殿が許され、五位以上の者と[[六位蔵人]]の者を合わせて[[殿上人]]と称した。神階においては、正六位が最下位となる。[[侍]]は朝廷の実務を担い貴族や諸大夫に仕える、位階六位どまりの侍品（さむらいほん）が元である。六位層は[[袍]]が[[縹]]色であることから、後には[[青侍]]と呼ばれた。
[[明治時代]]以降は、[[少佐]]の階級にある者などがこの位に叙せられた。
今日では[[日本の警察官|警察官]]では[[警視正]]、[[消防吏員]]では[[消防監]]などがこの位に叙せられる他、[[日本の地方議会|市町村議会]][[議長]]にあった者、特別施設や学校創立者、あるいは芸術家など業種等で功労ある者などが没後に叙せられる。
==外部リンク== * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/law/215IO0000000325 位階令] - [[e-Gov法令検索]] * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - 国立国会図書館近代デジタルライブラリー
{{日本の位階}} {{デフォルトソート:しようろくい}} [[Category:位階制度|しよう6い]]

Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Hō (robe)|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.

{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). revision.comment: Adding [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.

{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.

In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

{{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.comment: Removing this excess
revision.text:
[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.

It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. [[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.

The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.

revision.text:
[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). [[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

revision.text:
[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "従六位}}" to "従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}"
revision.text:
[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "正五位}}" to "正五位|1=Senior Fifth Rank}}"
revision.text:
[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.

revision.text:
[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.

The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.

However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.

[[Category:new pages]] {{Citation needed|date=August 2015}}
'''Shōrokui''' (正六位) is one of the {{ill|Ranks of Japan|ja|位階}} and {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}. It is positioned below {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank}} and above {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank}}. In terms of decorations, it corresponds to the Fifth Class of {{ill|Orders of Merit|ja|勲等}} and the Fifth Class of the {{ill|Order of the Golden Kite|ja|金鵄勲章}}.
Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the Sixth Rank was granted to {{ill|Kaike|ja|介}}, who served as the deputy governor of lower provinces. It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive

It was classified as a rank for {{ill|Jige|ja|地下人}} (non-court nobles) and was distinctly separate from the Fifth Rank or higher, which denoted nobility ({{ill|Tsūki|ja|通貴}}). Those holding the Sixth Rank were not permitted {{ill|Shōden|ja|昇殿}} (entry to the palace). However, {{ill|Kurōdo|ja|蔵人}} (Chamberlains) were exceptions; their roles allowed them palace access, and those of the Sixth Rank were called {{ill|Rokui Kurōdo|ja|六位蔵人}}, collectively referred to as {{ill|Tenjōbito|ja|殿上人}} alongside those of the Fifth Rank or higher.
In terms of {{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|lt=divine ranks|ja|神階}}, Shōrokui is the lowest rank. The {{ill|Samurai|ja|侍}} originated from the {{ill|Samuraihon|ja|侍品}} class, which consisted of individuals limited to the Sixth Rank who served as bureaucrats and aides to nobles. The Sixth Rank class was also referred to as {{ill|Aosamurai|ja|青侍}} (Blue Samurai) due to their {{ill|Po (clothing)|lt=Po robe|ja|袍|ja|袍}} being light blue in color.
In the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}} and later, individuals holding ranks such as {{ill|Major|ja|少佐}} were appointed to this position.
Today, Shōrokui is posthumously conferred to individuals such as {{ill|Keishisei|ja|警視正}} (Senior Police Superintendent) in the {{ill|Japanese police|ja|日本の警察官}}, {{ill|Shōbōkan|ja|消防監}} (Fire Superintendent) in the {{ill|Firefighting personnel|ja|消防吏員}}, chairs of {{ill|Municipal councils in Japan|ja|日本の地方議会}} (city, town, and village councils), founders of special institutions and schools, and artists who have made significant contributions in their respective fields.
== External links == * [https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/document?lawid=215IO0000000325 位階令] - {{ill|e-Gov Law Search|ja|e-Gov法令検索}} * [{{NDLDC|787949/168}} 官位相当表] - National Diet Library Digital Archive
